results.possibleIdentity: Larry Boyce (1946–1992) was an American artist and decorative painter known as the "Paint Pedaler." He was active in the early 1970s and is historically associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement through the Portland, Oregon chapter. Archives from Jack Eyerly, the founding member of the Portland E.A.T. chapter, contain documentation of Boyce's work and correspondence between 1971 and 1975. Boyce was celebrated for his mission to revive the craft of Victorian stenciling, specifically painting intricate friezes and patterns on the ceilings of historic buildings. He was a colorful figure in the art world who famously traveled over 200,000 miles across the United States on a bicycle to reach his project sites, often wearing a pith helmet and zebra-striped tights. His association with E.A.T. founders in the Pacific Northwest suggests he was part of the experimental art circles that integrated traditional craftsmanship with the alternative and collaborative ethos promoted by E.A.T. during that era.

David Boyce was a 'young artist' active in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is mentioned in oral histories from the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) as a collaborator and friend of major contemporary artists such as Jasper Johns, George Segal, and Robert Mapplethorpe. Around 1970, which was the height of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization's activity, Johns (a founding board member of E.A.T.) commissioned David Boyce to design the layout and catalogue for his print retrospective. David Boyce also assisted sculptor George Segal in identifying models for the 'Gay Liberation' monument commissioned in 1979 to commemorate the Stonewall uprising. His professional proximity to E.A.T.'s founders and his activity as a designer and artist in that specific milieu make him a plausible match for an artist named Boyce associated with the organization.
